coredata.dc:title: A new optical fiber biosensor for acetylcholine detection based on pH sensitive fluorescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yumei
Ding, Liyun
Zhang, Haowen
Wang, Pan
Li, Haijun
coredata.dc:description: Acetylcholine (Ach) is an important neurotransmitter and plays a vital role in the diagnosis and treatment of many neurological diseases. A new acetylcholine optical fiber biosensor is reported based on the nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots/ acetylcholinesterase (N-CQDs/AchE) compound, where AchE was immobilized on the surface of N-CQDs by the cross-linking method. N-CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method as fluorescent probe and can be responsible for pH value. The optical properties, morphology, particle size distribution and surface groups of N-CQDs were characterized by UV-Vis spectrophotometer, Fluorescence spectrometer, High-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), respectively. The relationship between acetylcholine chloride solution and fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/AchE complex was detected in detailed. Good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of acetylcholine from 20–200 µM and 1–100 nM with the lower detection limits of 16.28 µM and 0.29 nM, respectively. Compared with other sensors, this new optic fiber sensor immobilized (N-CQDs/AchE) complex is more simple and operable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fiber
Biosensor
N-CQDs
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Standard curve between the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and pH value
Preparation of complex sensitive film and sensor probe
Typical fluorescence response of the optical fiber sensor to Ach
The comprehensive performance of the optical fiber AchCl biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of optical fiber fluorescence experiment platform.

(A) TEM image of N-CQDs; (B) XRD pattern of N-CQDs; (C) FL spectra under different excitation wavelengths of N-CQDs; (D) the corresponding normalized spectra of N-CQDs; (E) UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation and emission spectrum of N-CQDs; (F) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CQDs dispersed in DHP-CA buffer solutions with different pH values (4.0–8.0); (B) A linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity and pH (4.0–8.0); (C) Zeta potential of N-CQDs at different pH values (4.0–8.0); (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs in DHP-CA solutions with different pH values (water, pH=4.0 and pH=8.0); (E) TEM image of the N-CQDs at pH= 8.0; (F) TEM image of the N-CQDs at pH= 4.0.
The cell inhibition of CQDs/AchE (100%) and the CQDs/AchE (50%) after 16/24 h.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the optical fiber sensor in the presence of AchCl solution with different concentrations ranging from 10 to 500 μM; (B) The AchCl detection time sequence diagram; (C) The calibration curve of the optical fiber sensor for the determination of AchCl ranging from 10 to 500 μM; (D) The calibration curve of the optical fiber sensor for the determination of AchCl ranging from 1 to 100 nM.

(a) Selectivity of different interferents of the optical fiber sensor for AchCl. I
0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of the AchCl optical fiber sensor absence and presence of interferents in test solution; (b) stability of optical fiber sensor.
Comparison of other various biosensors for detection of Ach.
Determination of AchCl in serum using the proposed sensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovery (%) is the ratio of the found AchCl concentration to the added AchCl concentration practical samples.

head.ce:title: A new optical fiber biosensor for acetylcholine detection based on pH sensitive fluorescent carbon quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acetylcholine (Ach) is an important neurotransmitter and plays a vital role in the diagnosis and treatment of many neurological diseases. A new acetylcholine optical fiber biosensor is reported based on the nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots/ acetylcholinesterase (N-CQDs/AchE) compound, where AchE was immobilized on the surface of N-CQDs by the cross-linking method. N-CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method as fluorescent probe and can be responsible for pH value. The optical properties, morphology, particle size distribution and surface groups of N-CQDs were characterized by UV-Vis spectrophotometer, Fluorescence spectrometer, High-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), respectively. The relationship between acetylcholine chloride solution and fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/AchE complex was detected in detailed. Good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of acetylcholine from 20–200 µM and 1–100 nM with the lower detection limits of 16.28 µM and 0.29 nM, respectively. Compared with other sensors, this new optic fiber sensor immobilized (N-CQDs/AchE) complex is more simple and operable.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetylcholine (Ach) as an important neurotransmitter in both the peripheral and central nervous systems was first exposed in 20th century [1]. Ach plays a significant role in many biological processes, such as memory, learning, attention and arousal [2–5]. Abnormal levels of Ach have a bearing on the nerve disorders, for example, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s, Huntington’s, progressive dementia, motor dysfunction, Schizophrenia, etc. [6–9]. It is of great significance to detecting of Ach with a sensitive, rapid, selective and accurate method. Various Ach measurements had been reported which included high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10–12], gas chromatography with mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [13,14], colorimetric detection [15,16], electrochemical sensors [2,4,17], colorimetry [20], fluorescent detection [21–24]. The HPLC, GC-MS methods can realize the quantitative analysis of Ach. However, these methods still need complex operation, high technical requirements, and insufficient accuracy and sensitivity [23]. The colorimetry has the disadvantages of color interference, high background signals and insufficient sensitivity [25]. Among all these sensors, fluorescent detection has excellent advantages due to the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, good reproducibility and superior stability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) wasa new class of nanomaterials and first found in 2004 by Xu et al. during the separation and purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [26]. Compared to the semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs are characterized with low toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost and chemical inertness [25]. And CQDs also have a lot of advantages compared to traditionally organic fluorophores, such as tunable fluorescence emissions, size-dependent, narrow emission spectra, resistance to photobleaching, long fluorescence lifetime, high chemical stability and excellent photostability [26–30]. CQDs were used extensively to chemical sensing, bio-sensing, bio-imaging, nano-medicine, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis fields thanks to the unique properties [33].

In this paper, a new optical fiber biosensor for Ach detection based on pH sensitive fluorescent carbon quantum dots was reported. The fiber optic probe was prepared by the method that AchE/CQDs/cellulose acetate (CA) film was coated on the end surface of large diameter quartz optical fiber with a diameter of 600 µm. Acetic acid and choline will produce when the hydrolysis of Ach by AchE with the decreasing of system pH value, which causes the quenching of fluorescence intensity. The optical fiber sensor had wide detection range, low LOD and good selectivity. Compared with other detection methods of Ach, the method of this paper only immobilized AchE which was more simple, smaller, free electromagnetic interference and is expected to realize the real-time online detection of acetylcholine.

Sodium ascorbate (≥99.0%) was purchased from Aladdin. o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5%), Acetylcholine chloride (AchCl, ≥99%) and Acetylcholinesterase from electric eel (AchE, 200 U/g) were supplied by Macklin. Citric acid monohydrate (AR), Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (AR) and sulfuric acid (95–98%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) (AR) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water was made by the Hitech-K flow water purification system.

N-CQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. 0.3244 g o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5%) and 0.1981 g sodium ascorbate were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water and treated at 180 °C for 9 h. A dark brown solution was obtained after reaction finished and cooled to room temperature. The solution was then filtered with a 0.22 µm pores diameter membrane filter to remove the larger particles. The filtrate was dialysis 24 h and filtered with a 0.22 µm pores diameter membrane filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve between the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and pH value
ce:section.ce:para: Purified N-CQDs 300 µl was added to 1.2 ml disodium hydrogen phosphate (DHP)-citric acid (CA) buffers with a variety pH value (pH 4.0–8.0) [18]. Then the fluorescence spectra were recorded after 5 min in the wavelength range of 300–700 nm at an excitation wavelength of 473 nm. All the measurements were repeated three times and then averaged. The fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs were used to evaluate the pH value in DHP-CA buffers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of complex sensitive film and sensor probe
ce:section.ce:para: The AchE/CQDs complex was prepared by cross-linking method. 10 mg EDC and 2 ml purified CQDs were stir 10 min in an ice water bath. Then 110 µl AchE (1 mg/ml) was added to the mixture and continue stirred for 4 h. The preparation process of AchE/CQDs/CA complex sensitive film was as follows: 0.3 g cellulose acetate was dissolved in 3 ml acetone under magnetic stirring. Then 900 µl AchE/CQDs complex was added and mixed evenly in an ice water bath for 6 h. The quartz optical fiber with a diameter of 600 µm was selected. Firstly, the fibers length was 10 cm and the end face of fibers were polished smoothly with sandpaper. Secondly, the fibers were washed by deionized water and ethanol for 3 times and dry at room temperature. Thirdly, the AchE/CQDs/CA coating solution was coating on the end face of fiber by dip-coating method through a vertical coating lifting machine. Finally, the sensitive film coated fibers were stored in refrigerator at 4 °C overnight.

The schematic of optical fiber fluorescence experiment platform for the detection of Ach was shown in Fig. 1. The high-stable incident laser was provided by a 473 nm violet diode laser. The incident laser was divided into two parts of laser by dichroic mirror after through the attenuator. One parts of the laser was gauging by optical power meter. Another parts of the laser were reflected by the spectroscope for sensing. After focusing by 10x microscope objective lens, the laser was coupled into an optical fiber core on adjusting the 3D platform. The laser was traveled along the core to the other end of the fiber and excited the CQDs on the sensitive film to emit fluorescence. The fluorescence signal was transmitted in reverse along the optical fiber and through the objective lens, dichroscope, the high-pass filter and eventually received and processed by the Ocean Optics QE 6500 spectrometer and the computer.

The mechanism of Ach detection was that the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by producing acetic acid of the hydrolysis of Ach and AchE. The concentration of Ach can be detected by the change of fluorescence intensity since the dependent relationships between the concentration of Ach and quenching degree of fluorescence intensity. The process of Ach detection was as follows: the prepared sensor probe firstly fixed on the 3D platform (Fig. 1) and the optical power of 473 nm violet diode laser was about 160 µW, the integration time was 900 ms. And then the fluorescence intensity of the probe reached the maximum by controlled the XYZ axis fine tuning lifting optical platform. Thirdly, dipping into a 1.5 ml glass bottle which was full of Ach solution; the fluorescence signal was collected and analyzed by spectrometer. A series of standard Ach solutions with different concentrations were measured to obtain the calibration curves of the Ach biosensor.

The morphology and particle size distribution CQDs was characterized by a JEM-2100 F Transmission electron microscope (TEM). The FTIR spectra were recorded in the range of 600–4000 cm−1 using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was collected by a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Fluorescence was measured by a F-4500 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained using a ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Zeta potential were characterized by a Malvern Nano-ZS Zeta potential meter. The 473 nm violet diode laser (MBL–PN–473–150 mW) and QE65000 spectrometer (Ocean Optics) quipped with a back-thinned CCD detector were employed to construct the optical sensing platform.

The cytotoxicity of N-CQDs was assessed by a cell counting kit-8 (CCK8). Briefly, SH-SY5Y cells were inoculated into 96 well cell plates at 8000 per well and incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 ℃ overnight. Add 20 µl/well configured CQDS ache into the cell plate. The cell plate is incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 ℃ for 16/24 h in the dark. After incubation, the cells were added with CCK8, 10ul per well and incubated in 37 ℃ 5% CO2 incubator for 1 h. The absorbance at 450 nm wavelength was measured on envision and the inhibition rate was calculated,
Cell inhibition rate
%
=
1 −
OD
S
OD
NC
× 100 %
where ODS refers to absorbance value of sample holes; ODNC refers to absorbance value of negative holes.

The N-CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and sodium ascorbate at 180 °C for 9 h. As shown in Fig. 2(A), TEM result illustrated that the N-CQDs were monodispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles and the average size was 5.6 nm with a narrow size distribution from 3.95 to 7.65 nm (the right inset of Fig. 2 (A)). The high-resolution TEM image in the left inset of Fig. 2 (A) demonstrated that the well-resolved lattice spacing of N-CQDs is 0.1934 nm which is in good conformance with the graphite lattice plane. [18] The XRD pattern of N-CQDs was shown in Fig. 2 (B). There was a board diffraction peak with a center at around 24° which further verified that the graphitic structure of N-CQDs [32].

The optical properties of N-CQDs were characterized by fluorescence and UV–vis spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 (C), (D) and (E). Fig. 2 (C) displayed the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. The FL intensity first increased and then decreased with an increase in excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 390 nm. The FL intensity reached the maximum when the excitation wavelength was 350 nm. And the peaks of emission peak redshifted from 415 nm to 460 nm. The FL intensity had little change with an increase in excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 490 nm. The peaks of emission peak redshifted from 560 nm to 570 nm. As Fig. 2 (D), the corresponding normalized spectra of N-CQDs showed. These results proved that the N-CQDs had excitation-dependent properties as reported in other literatures [33]. The characteristic was mainly because of the different surface groups on the N-CQDs optical selection of nanoparticles with different sizes which was called quantum effect. As shown in Fig. 2 (E), the UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs exhibited three bands at 312 nm, 355 nm and 455 nm. The absorption peaks centered at 312 nm and 355 nm were attributed to the n-π * transition of CO and CN bond [34,35]. The visible absorption bond which peak centered at 455 nm was belonged to the hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs. Moreover, it was obvious that the N-CQDs possessed excitation and emission peaks at 350 nm and 440 nm, respectively.

As seen in Fig. 2 (F), the FTIR spectra showed the surface functional groups of N-CQDs. The broad absorption band at about 3430 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H. [35] The band at about 2973 cm−1 was characteristic of CH2 [36]. The distinct bands located at about 1650 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO, C-N [35,37]. These results demonstrated that the surface of N-CQDs presence various functional groups. And the groups like amino and hydroxyl were beneficial to improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the N-CQDs in aqueous systems [33].

The pH sensitive behavior of N-CQDs was detailed investigated in this paper. As illustrated in Fig. 3 (A), with pH values changing from 4.0 to 8.0, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs excitation at 473 nm increased continuously. A pH calibration curve in DHP-CA buffer solutions was plotted in Fig. 3 (B), which displayed a good linear relationship in rang of 5.4–7.4. To investigate the pH sensitive reason of N-CQDs, the Zeta potential, UV–vis spectra and TEM images were measured.

As shown in Fig. 3 (C), the Zeta potential changed from − 4.0 mV to − 21.5 mV as the pH value from 4.0 to 8.0, which is in accordance with the phenomenon of pH-induced deprotonation of the amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs [38,39]. The decrease of the absolute value of zeta potential led to the decreased stability of the whole system and then the agglomeration of carbon dots, which was corresponded to the TEM image at pH= 4.0. Fig. 3 (D) showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs at pH= 8.0, 4.0 and water. The absorption peaks at 312 nm and 355 nm had almost no change. The absorption peak at 455 nm was redshifted to 476 nm with decreasing pH value from pH= 8.0 to pH= 4.0 due to the electronic effect of the surface groups. [40] As shown in Fig. 3 (E) and (F), the average size of N-CQDs at pH= 8.0, 4.0 was 3.8 nm and 6.59 nm, respectively. There was obviously aggregation of N-CQDs in acidic environment. In conclusion, the variation of FL intensity of N-CQDs under different pH values should be attributed to the protonation and aggregation of N-CQDs in acid solutions.

The CCK8 assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CQDs. The results were shown in Fig. 4, the cell inhibition of CQDs/AchE (100%) and the CQDs/AchE (50%) diluted to two times on SH-SY5Y cells after 16 h and 24 h. The cell inhibition of CQDs/AchE (100%) for 16 h and 24 h were 49.32%, 51.70%, respectively and that of CQDs/AchE (50%) was 31.50%, 35.59%, respectively. The inhibition rate of N-CQDs ached at low concentration was small. In general, the results showed that the cytotoxicity of carbon dots was low and in an acceptable range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Typical fluorescence response of the optical fiber sensor to Ach
ce:section.ce:para:
AchCl solutions were prepared by dissolving acetylcholine in ultrapure water. The pH value of ultrapure water was 6.64 by using the Thunder Magnetic PHS-3 C pH meter. The effects of pH and temperature changes on the fluorescence intensity of the sensor probes without AchE in different concentrations of AchCl solutions were discussed in Supplementary material. The results showed that the temperature and pH value of the samples have no effect on the detection process. AchCl solutions with a series different concentrations ranging from 10 µM/L to 500 µM/L and 1 nmol/L to 100 nmol/L were prepared and measured by the 3D optical fiber sensing platform. As presented in Fig. 5(A), the increase of AchCl concentrations result in the fluorescence intensity decreased because the decreased pH value of the system with the acetic acid produced with the hydrolysis of AchCl by AChE. The real-time fluorescence response of optical fiber sensor to AchCl ranging from 10 µM/L to 500 µM/L were test and shown in Fig. 5(B). As a result, the fluorescence intensity dramatically decreased in 1 min and then gradually stabilized within 5 min when the optical fiber probe was dipped into the tested solution. The fluorescence intensity of the probe had a little decreased to a certain extent before reaching complete stability, because the diffusion of AchCl into CA film and the progress of enzymatic reaction take a certain time. The fluorescence intensity of the probe can be restored to a certain extent when the probe was removed from the solution to be measured, which was consistent with Fig. 5(A). The modified Stern-Volmer plot of I0/I values versus the concentrations of AchCl was shown in Fig. 5(C), the concentrations of AchCl ranging from 10 µM/L to 500 µM/L) and Fig. 5(D), the concentrations of AchCl ranging from 1 nmol/L to 100 nmol/L, respectively, where the I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of the AchCl optical fiber sensor absence and presence of AchCl in test solution [40]. From the plot of Fig. 5(C), the linear range was determined to be 20–200 µM/L with the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3, S and N represent the standard deviation of the blank signal and the slope of the linear fitting line, respectively) was 16.28 µM/L, where the calibration test results were described by the equation of
I
0
/
I = 0.00288 x + 1.07771
(R2 =0.99398). For Fig. 5(D), the range of the fluorescence intensity ratio exhibited linear dependence with the AchCl concentrations was divided into two parts, 1–10 nM/L and 10–100 nM/L, and the fitting equation were described as
I
0
/ I = 0.02207 x + 1.04907
(R2= 0.99707) and
I
0
/ I = 0.00259 x + 1.25586
(R2= 0.98709), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.29 nM. In conclusion, the optical fiber sensor of AchCl has the highest sensitivity in the range of 1–10 nM. The limit of quantification (LOQ) is the minimum concentration of a substance that can be quantitatively measured in the specific product with an acceptable level of accuracy and precision. In order to increase the credibility of data, LOQ is expressed as 10 times of LOD, Therefore, the LOQ of the sensor is 162.8 µΜ and 2.9 nM, respectively. According to the literature reports the normal concentration of Ach in human blood is 8.66 ± 1.02 nM, the optical fiber probe reported in this article can detect AchCl concentrations in human body [41].

Table 1 showed the comparison with reported detection of Ach by fluorescence analysis. Compared with other electrochemical and fluorescence methods, the acetylcholine optical fiber biosensor prepared in this paper has a wider detection range and lower detection limit, and the applicable range and field can be further expanded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comprehensive performance of the optical fiber AchCl biosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Selectivity and stability are the important index to evaluate the comprehensive performance of sensors. We investigated the selectivity of the optic fiber sensor to different interferents including Sodium chloride, Potassium chloride, Calcium chloride, Sodium ascorbate, Serine, Glycine, Histidine, Glucose, Glutamate, which the assay toward AchCl (100 nM) over other interferents (each 1000 nM). Fig. 6(a) indicated that the sensing system was quite selective toward AchCl. The change of fluorescence of interferents (I
0
-I / I＜0.06) was insignificant compared with that of AchCl (I
0
-I / I = 0.409) in the sensing system, which indicated that this fiber-optic AchCl sensor has relatively high selectivity. Fig. 6(b) was the stability of optical fiber sensor. To estimate the stability of optical fiber sensor, we put the sensor probe in ultrapure water and air for three times, and the fluorescence intensity of the sensor is basically stable.

To estimate the applicability and feasibility of the optical fiber sensor, a standard addition method with different AchCl concentration (0 µM, 60 µM, 80 µM, 100 µM, 120 µM, 140 µM, 5 nM) added in serum was used. The results were shown in Table 2. The recovery rate of each AchCl concentration is between 93.58% and 102.72%, which were in good agreement with the added AchCl. It suggested that the optical fiber sensor had excellent practical application value.

In this paper, pH sensitive fluorescent carbon quantum dots were applied to the detection of acetylcholine. A N-CQDs/CA/AchE complex sensitive film with enzyme activity and fluorescence characteristics was prepared by immobilized acetylcholinesterase on the fluorescent carbon quantum dots, and then coated in cellulose acetate. A fluorescent probe for the specific detection of acetylcholine was prepared by coating the composite sensitive film on the end face of the optical fiber with a core diameter of 600 µm by impregnation method. Acetic acid based on enzymatic reaction leads to the decrease of environmental pH and quenching the fluorescence of carbon dots. The optical fiber biosensor has the advantages of fast response, high sensitivity and wide detection range. It has broad application prospects in the prevention and treatment of Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia and other diseases.

Yumei Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Liyun Ding: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Haowen Zhang: Visualization, Pan Wang: Methodology, Haijun Li: Resources. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51878524), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WUT: 2021Ⅲ028JC) and the Hubei Outstanding Youth Fund Project in 2021 (2021CFA041), the Huhhot Science & Technology Plan project (No. 2020-Ke Ji Xing Meng-National Innovation Center-12).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132268.

ce:section.ce:para:

Microchem. J.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yumei Zhang is now a Doctoral candidate in Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan. Her research interest is in optical fiber biosensors.

Liyun Ding received her Ph. D in materials science from Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, in 2007. She is now a research scientist in National Engineering Research Center of Fiber Optic Sensing Technology and Networks in the Wuhan University of Technology. Her research interest is in field of the optical fiber chemical and biosensors.
Haowen Zhang is now a graduate student in Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan. His research interest is in optical fiber chemical biosensors.
Pan Wang is now a graduate student in Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan.

Haijun Li is a professor of Veterinary Medicine College of Inner Mongolia Agricultural University. His main research direction is basic veterinary science, which mainly studies the mechanism of mammalian oogenesis and embryonic development.
